name: Sasaki Kojiro
description: Sasaki Kojiro, also known as Ganryū, was a prominent and enigmatic swordsman in Japanese history, particularly during the late Sengoku and early Edo periods. While much of his life remains shrouded in legend, he is celebrated as a master of the nodachi, a long and formidable sword. He developed a unique and devastating sword technique known as the 'Turning Swallow Cut' (Tsubame Gaeshi), rumored to be so swift and precise that it could strike a swallow in mid-flight. Kojiro's origins are somewhat vague, with various accounts placing his birth in different regions. He is believed to have founded his own kenjutsu school, Ganryū, which emphasized the use of the nodachi. His skill and reputation led him to numerous duels, solidifying his status as one of the most dangerous swordsmen of his time. Sasaki Kojiro's legacy is primarily defined by his legendary duel against Miyamoto Musashi on Ganryū Island (now known as Funashima). Despite Kojiro wielding a significantly longer sword, Musashi emerged victorious, though the circumstances surrounding the duel and Kojiro's death remain debated. He is remembered as a formidable warrior and a master swordsman, whose skills continue to inspire awe and fascination.
subtitle: Master Swordsman of the Nodachi

keyMyths.title: The Turning Swallow Cut
keyMyths.description: Sasaki Kojiro's signature technique, Tsubame Gaeshi, was said to be so fast and precise that it could strike a swallow in mid-flight. Its mechanics are debated, but it involved a rapid series of upward and downward strikes, creating a deadly defense. This technique showcases his incredible skill and control.

keyMyths.title: The Duel on Ganryū Island
keyMyths.description: The duel between Sasaki Kojiro and Miyamoto Musashi is the defining event of Kojiro's life. Musashi arrived late, allegedly to unnerve Kojiro, and defeated him using a specially crafted wooden sword, or bokken, despite Kojiro's superior reach. The details of the fight are disputed, but its impact is undeniable.

keyMyths.title: Monohoshi Zao - The Drying Pole
keyMyths.description: Kojiro's nodachi, known as Monohoshi Zao, translates to 'Drying Pole,' a testament to its exceptional length. Its size and weight made it a difficult weapon to master, but in Kojiro's hands, it became a deadly instrument. It symbolized his unique fighting style.

sources.author: Kenji Tokitsu
sources.work: Miyamoto Musashi: His Life and Writings
sources.description: A scholarly examination of Miyamoto Musashi, which also discusses his famous duel with Sasaki Kojiro.
sources.text: A scholarly examination of Miyamoto Musashi, which also discusses his famous duel with Sasaki Kojiro.
